children.name: Desmodus
children.description: Vampire bats — the only mammals that feed exclusively on blood (hematophagy). The common vampire bat feeds primarily on large mammals, landing near sleeping cattle, horses, or other animals and using heat-sensing pits to locate blood vessels close to the surface, then making a small incision with razor-sharp incisors and lapping the flowing blood. Anticoagulants in the saliva (called draculin) keep the blood flowing. A vampire bat that fails to feed on any given night will die within 60 hours; to prevent this, well-fed bats in a roost regularly regurgitate blood to feed hungry roost-mates — one of the most sophisticated examples of reciprocal altruism in any non-human animal.

children.name: Desmodus rotundus
children.description: Feeds almost exclusively on cattle blood; can walk and leap on the ground with surprising agility to approach prey. The draculin anticoagulant in its saliva is under investigation as a potential stroke treatment.

children.name: Desmodus draculae
children.description: An extinct giant vampire bat known from Pleistocene fossils in South America; wingspan estimated at 50 cm.

children.name: Desmodus draculae
children.description: An extinct giant vampire bat known from Pleistocene fossils; 30% larger than living Desmodus.

children.name: Desmodus rotundus
children.description: The most famous of the vampire bats. It feeds primarily on the blood of domestic livestock, using heat-sensing pits on its nose to locate blood vessels close to the skin. Its saliva contains a powerful anticoagulant, desmoteplase, which has been studied for use in treating human stroke patients.

children.name: Desmodus draculae
children.description: An extinct or possibly still extant giant vampire bat from the Pleistocene of South America. Its fossil remains have been found in Venezuela and Brazil. Some cryptozoologists believe it may still survive in remote areas.

children.name: Desmodus rotundus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Desmodus draculae
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Desmodus archaeodaptes
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Diaemus
children.description: The White-winged Vampire Bat — a rare and specialised vampire bat that feeds primarily on bird blood rather than mammal blood, unlike its more familiar relative. It has evolved specific behaviours for approaching roosting birds, including walking on the dorsal surface of a bird and biting the base of a wing feather to access blood vessels.

children.name: Diaemus youngi
children.description: The only vampire bat to feed primarily on bird blood, with specialised behaviours for approaching roosting poultry and wild birds. Distinguished by white wing margins and a large rostral gland that may play a role in communicating with prey. Far rarer and less studied than the Common Vampire Bat.

children.name: Diphylla
children.description: The Hairy-legged Vampire Bat — one of three true vampire bat species and the most morphologically distinct, with a short, broad muzzle, large eyes, and a leaf-nosed face unlike the other vampires. It feeds exclusively on bird blood and is found in humid tropical and subtropical forest.

children.name: Diphylla ecaudata
children.description: A specialised bird-blood vampire bat of humid tropical forest from Texas to Brazil, distinguished from other vampires by its densely furred interfemoral membrane and its deep, fork-shaped lower incisors adapted for avian skin. It is less frequently encountered than the Common Vampire Bat and is highly dependent on intact forest.

children.name: Glossophaga
children.description: Nectar bats — hovering specialists with elongated snouts and brush-tipped tongues longer than their skulls that allow them to access the nectar of deep tubular flowers. They are the primary pollinators of many cactus and agave species, including the blue agave used to make tequila. Losing nectar bat populations can collapse entire plant communities — some desert plants depend entirely on these bats for pollination. Nectar bats have the fastest tongues relative to body size of any mammal, lapping nectar at up to 15 times per second.

children.name: Glossophaga soricina
children.description: The most widespread nectar bat in Latin America. Critical pollinator of more than 150 plant species including agaves, cacti, and bananas. Can hover like a hummingbird for up to 1.5 seconds while feeding.

children.name: Glossophaga commissarisi
children.description: A widespread nectar bat of humid lowland forests from Mexico to Bolivia, closely resembling Pallas's Long-tongued Bat but distinguished by a slightly shorter, broader skull. Named after Raymond F. Commissaris; it pollinates many of the same flowering plants as its sister species and is a major seed disperser for small fruits.

children.name: Glossophaga longirostris
children.description: A long-nosed nectar bat of dry and semi-arid scrublands in Venezuela, Colombia, and the Lesser Antilles, with an even more elongated rostrum than related species. It is a key pollinator of columnar cacti and agaves in the drier parts of its range.

children.name: Glossophaga leachii
children.description: A small nectar bat of Pacific-slope dry forests and scrublands from Mexico to Costa Rica, one of the greyer-furred members of the genus. It pollinates columnar cacti and flowering trees in seasonally dry tropical habitats.

children.name: Glossophaga mutica
children.description: A highland Glossophaga from the Venezuelan Andes; exceptionally long tongue for nectar feeding.

children.name: Glossophaga antillarum
children.description: A Jamaican endemic; vital pollinator of columnar cacti and agaves.

children.name: Glossophaga longirostris
children.description: A nectar bat with an elongated snout; pollinates columnar cacti in arid regions.

children.name: Glossophaga morenoi
children.description: A recently described species from the Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Glossophaga soricina
children.description: A small nectar bat with an exceptionally long tongue, extending up to 8 cm (nearly 1.5 times its body length). It hovers like a hummingbird while feeding on nectar.

children.name: Glossophaga commissarisi
children.description: A small nectar bat of Central and northern South America. Its tongue is proportionally longer than that of G. soricina.

children.name: Glossophaga commissarisi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Glossophaga leachii
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Glossophaga longirostris
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Glossophaga morenoi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Glossophaga soricina
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Anoura
children.description: Tailless bats — long-tongued nectar bats of Andean and montane Neotropical forests, important pollinators of high-elevation flowering plants including many orchids and bromeliads. Anoura geoffroyi has one of the longest tongues relative to body size of any mammal.

children.name: Anoura geoffroyi
children.description: One of the most widespread montane nectar bats in the Americas, ranging from Mexico to Bolivia in cloud forest, mountain scrub, and forest edges. Named after the French naturalist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire; it has an exceptionally long tongue and is an important pollinator of high-elevation plants including many orchids.

children.name: Anoura cultrata
children.description: A large tailless bat of Andean cloud forests from Colombia to Bolivia, one of the bigger members of the genus. It forages for nectar at flowering plants along forest edges and in clearings at elevations between 1,000 and 2,500 m.

children.name: Anoura luismanueleli
children.description: A recently described highland species from Colombia; named after a conservationist.

children.name: Anoura latidens
children.description: Found from Venezuela to Peru; inhabits cloud forests at 1500-3000 m in elevation.

children.name: Lonchophylla
children.description: Sword-nosed bats — specialised nectar feeders of tropical South and Central America with a distinctive elongated rostrum and tongue. They complement Glossophaga and Anoura in nectar-feeding guilds, often serving as pollinators for different flower types.

children.name: Lonchophylla robusta
children.description: A robust, orange-tinged nectar bat of humid lowland and foothill forests from Nicaragua to Peru, one of the larger sword-nosed bats. It feeds on nectar and pollen but also takes insects and soft fruit, making it more ecologically flexible than some nectar specialists.

children.name: Lonchophylla mordax
children.description: A small sword-nosed bat found in the Atlantic Forest and Cerrado of eastern Brazil, a nectar and pollen specialist that pollinates many native plants. It is among the more poorly known Lonchophylla species and faces habitat loss pressure as the Atlantic Forest continues to contract.

children.name: Lonchophylla alkalescens
children.description: A small nectar bat from the Colombian Andes with pale yellow belly.

children.name: Lonchophylla cadenai
children.description: A recently discovered species from the Choco region of Ecuador.

children.name: Lonchophylla intermedia
children.description: A species found from Colombia to Peru; pollinates night-blooming plants.

children.name: Lichonycteris
children.description: Dark long-tongued bats — small, dark-furred nectar feeders of humid lowland forests in Central and South America, closely related to Lonchophylla. They inhabit the understorey of lowland tropical forest and are among the smaller members of the nectar-feeding guild.

children.name: Lichonycteris obscura
children.description: A small, dark-furred nectar bat of humid lowland forests from Belize to Bolivia, among the most poorly known of the Neotropical nectar feeders. It feeds on small flowers in the understorey and is rarely encountered due to its low-flying habits close to dense vegetation.

children.name: Artibeus
children.description: Jamaican fruit bats and their relatives — robust, short-faced bats built for processing hard tropical fruits. Artibeus jamaicensis is one of the most abundant Neotropical bats and a crucial seed disperser for figs, wild avocados, and hundreds of other forest trees. They carry fruit to eating roosts away from the parent tree, dropping seeds in forest gaps where they have the best chance of germination. Artibeus bats can carry fruit up to 50% of their own body weight and process a mango-sized fruit in under a minute.

children.name: Artibeus jamaicensis
children.description: Among the most abundant mammals in lowland Neotropical forest. Disperses seeds of figs and forest trees up to 2 km from the parent tree. Has a distinctive white facial stripe.

children.name: Artibeus lituratus
children.description: The largest member of the genus and one of the largest Neotropical bats, with a wingspan approaching 45 cm. A major seed disperser of large figs and forest fruits, it is abundant in forest edges, second growth, and urban parks from Mexico to Argentina. Four white facial stripes distinguish it from its relatives.

children.name: Artibeus obscurus
children.description: A medium-sized fruit bat of Amazonian and Atlantic Forest, dark-furred and similar in appearance to Artibeus jamaicensis but lacking bold facial stripes. It is an important disperser of figs and other pioneer plant fruits in Amazonian and riverine habitats.

children.name: Artibeus planirostris
children.description: A robust South American fruit bat of savannas, dry forests, and forest edges from Venezuela to Argentina, adapted to a wider variety of fruit types than its rainforest relatives. It is common in both natural and disturbed habitats and helps maintain seed dispersal services in fragmented landscapes.

children.name: Artibeus phaeotis
children.description: One of the smallest Artibeus, a compact fruit bat of humid lowland and foothill forests from Mexico to northwestern South America. Despite its small size it handles surprisingly large fruits relative to its body; it constructs tent-like leaf roosts by biting the lateral veins of large leaves.

children.name: Artibeus watsoni
children.description: A small fruit bat of humid lowland forests from Mexico to Ecuador, notable for its sophisticated tent-making behaviour: it modifies palm leaves by biting strategic vein cuts to create a tent-like roost shared by a male and his harem. One of the most intensively studied tent-making bats.

children.name: Artibeus toltecus
children.description: A small, common fruit bat of Central American humid forests from Mexico to Panama, one of several small Artibeus that focus on figs and soft fruits of understorey plants. It roosts in small groups under palm fronds or broad leaves.

children.name: Artibeus concolor
children.description: A small uniform brown Artibeus from the Amazon and Guiana Shield.

children.name: Artibeus frater
children.description: Found in the Tumbesian region of Ecuador and Peru; distinctive pale facial stripes.

children.name: Artibeus glandulifer
children.description: A rare Artibeus from Suriname and French Guiana; prominent facial scent glands.

children.name: Artibeus amplexus
children.description: A small fruit bat endemic to the Lesser Antilles.

children.name: Artibeus concolor
children.description: A uniform brown fruit bat of the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Artibeus fimbriatus
children.description: A large fruit bat from the Brazilian Atlantic Forest; distinctive fringe on uropatagium.

children.name: Artibeus gnomus
children.description: A tiny Artibeus from Amazonia; the smallest in the genus.

children.name: Artibeus watsoni
children.description: A small Central American species; named after Thomas Watson.

children.name: Artibeus jamaicensis
children.description: One of the most common bats in Central America and the Caribbean. Its leaf-shaped nose and prominent facial stripes make it easily recognisable. An important disperser of fig seeds.

children.name: Artibeus lituratus
children.description: A large Artibeus with distinctive white facial stripes. It is one of the most common bats of lowland Neotropical forests.

children.name: Artibeus obscurus
children.description: A dark, medium-sized Artibeus of the Amazon basin. Its lack of facial stripes distinguishes it from A. lituratus.

children.name: Artibeus amplus
children.description: A large Artibeus of the Guiana Shield and northern Amazon. One of the largest members of the genus.

children.name: Artibeus amplus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus concolor
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus fimbriatus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus fraterculus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus glaucus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus hirsutus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus incomitatus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus jamaicensis
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus lituratus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus obscurus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus phaeotis
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus planirostris
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus rosenbergi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Artibeus toltecus
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira
children.description: Yellow-shouldered bats — diverse fruit bats of Neotropical forests named for the yellowish or orange shoulder patches found in males of many species. Sturnira are critical seed dispersers for Solanum (nightshades), Piper (peppers), and other pioneer plants that rapidly colonise forest gaps; in disturbed forests they are often the dominant seed-dispersing bat. With over 20 species, the genus spans a wide range of elevations from lowland Amazonia to Andean cloud forest.

children.name: Sturnira lilium
children.description: One of the most abundant bats in disturbed Neotropical forests, ranking among the most frequent visitors to forest-edge fruit plants. A specialist on Solanum and Piper fruits, it is a key agent of secondary forest regeneration wherever primary forest has been cleared. Males have distinctive yellow-orange shoulder glands.

children.name: Sturnira bogotensis
children.description: A cloud-forest fruit bat of the Andes of Colombia and Venezuela, inhabiting montane forests between 1,500 and 3,000 m elevation. It feeds on the small fruits of montane Solanum and related plants and is an important seed disperser in Andean secondary vegetation.

children.name: Sturnira tildae
children.description: A lowland and foothill forest fruit bat ranging from Trinidad and Tobago south through the Amazon basin, closely related to the Little Yellow-shouldered Bat and inhabiting similar disturbed and edge habitats. Males have conspicuous tawny shoulder glands active during the breeding season.

children.name: Sturnira erythromos
children.description: An Andean fruit bat of dense cloud forest and scrub from Colombia to Bolivia, recognised by its distinctively hairy face and ears which help it deal with the cool, mist-laden conditions of its high-altitude habitat. It is a key disperser of fruits of the genus Solanum above 2,000 m.

children.name: Sturnira magna
children.description: The largest species in the genus, a robust fruit bat of Amazonian lowland and foothill forests in Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela. It takes larger fruits than its smaller relatives and may play a disproportionate seed-dispersal role for larger-seeded pioneer plants.

children.name: Sturnira luisi
children.description: A medium-sized yellow-shouldered bat of humid lowland and foothill forests from Costa Rica to Venezuela, named after Luis García-Lamas. It inhabits the edge zones between forest and cultivation, where it plays an important role dispersing seeds of pioneer shrubs into degraded areas.

children.name: Sturnira hondurensis
children.description: A recently described yellow-shouldered bat endemic to the highlands of Honduras and adjacent Guatemala, inhabiting pine-oak and cloud forest between 1,000 and 2,500 m. Its discovery as a distinct species highlights how much bat diversity in Central American mountains remains undescribed.

children.name: Sturnira angipolii
children.description: A Colombian highland species; named after the collector; one of many Sturnira in the Andes.

children.name: Sturnira aratathomasi
children.description: A large Sturnira from the Cordillera Central of Colombia; named after Oldfield Thomas.

children.name: Sturnira burnettense
children.description: A newly described species from Ecuadorian cloud forests.

children.name: Sturnira magna
children.description: A large Sturnira from the Andes; distinctive yellow shoulder patches in males.

children.name: Sturnira eriana
children.description: A recently described species from the Ecuadorian Andes.

children.name: Sturnira parvidens
children.description: A small Central American species with minute teeth.

children.name: Sturnira aratathomasi
children.description: A large highland species from the Colombian Andes.

children.name: Sturnira lilium
children.description: A common fruit bat from Mexico to Argentina. Males have distinctive yellowish shoulder patches. One of the most frequently captured bats in mist-net surveys throughout its range.

children.name: Sturnira erian
children.description: A large Sturnira of the Andean cloud forests. Its dense fur and relatively large size are adaptations to cooler montane environments.

children.name: Sturnira luisi
children.description: A yellow-shouldered bat of the Pacific slopes of Ecuador and Peru. Named after the mammalogist Luis.

children.name: Sturnira tildae
children.description: A common yellow-shouldered bat of the Amazon basin, named after Tilda, the wife of the describing naturalist.

children.name: Sturnira aratathomasi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira bidens
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira bogotensis
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira erythromos
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira lilium
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira ludovici
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira luisi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira magna
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira mistratensis
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira mordax
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira nana
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira oporaphilum
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira perla
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira sorianoi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Sturnira tildae
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia
children.description: Short-tailed fruit bats — small to medium phyllostomids that are among the most abundant mammals in Neotropical lowland forest. They specialise in the fruit of pioneer plants — particularly Piper (pepper plants) — and are therefore among the first seed dispersers to recolonise forest gaps and disturbed areas. In heavily deforested landscapes, Carollia bats may be the only remaining seed dispersers capable of beginning forest regeneration. Their ecological importance in disturbed habitats makes them a flagship species for the conservation value of bats.

children.name: Carollia perspicillata
children.description: Often the single most abundant bat species at Neotropical lowland sites. Specialises in Piper fruits. Has become a model species in bat research due to its tolerance of captivity and active breeding in labs.

children.name: Carollia brevicauda
children.description: A widespread short-tailed bat of humid lowland and montane forest from Mexico to Peru, closely related to Seba's Short-tailed Bat but with a broader ecological range extending to higher elevations. It is a prolific disperser of Piper seeds in montane secondary vegetation.

children.name: Carollia castanea
children.description: The smallest of the common Carollia species, a chestnut-brown bat of humid lowland forests from Honduras to Bolivia. It is among the most abundant bats in lowland Amazonian sites and is a primary disperser of small-seeded Piper species in the understorey.

children.name: Carollia subrufa
children.description: A rufous-furred short-tailed bat of Pacific-slope dry and moist forests from Mexico to Costa Rica, the most drought-tolerant member of the genus. It persists in seasonally dry scrubland where other Carollia are absent and is an important disperser of Piper in seasonally dry forest.

children.name: Carollia benkeithi
children.description: A small Carollia from the lowland Amazon; separated from C. perspicillata by molecular data.

children.name: Carollia monteroae
children.description: A Bolivian endemic; discovered in the Yungas cloud forests.

children.name: Carollia persicillata
children.description: A common fruit bat of Amazonia; distinctive silky fur and stubby tail.

children.name: Carollia mansu
children.description: A recently described species from the Peruvian Amazon; named after the local word for bat.

children.name: Carollia brevicauda
children.description: A small Carollia with a very short tail; widespread in Neotropical lowlands.

children.name: Carollia subrufa
children.description: A Central American species; grey-brown with a slightly rufous venter.

children.name: Carollia perspicillata
children.description: One of the most abundant mammals in the Neotropics. Its simple leaf nose and short tail are typical of the genus. A key disperser of pioneer plant seeds.

children.name: Carollia brevicauda
children.description: A small Carollia with a very short tail. Found across the Amazon and Orinoco basins.

children.name: Carollia castanea
children.description: A reddish-brown Carollia of the western Amazon. Its chestnut fur colour is distinctive.

children.name: Carollia benkeithi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia brevicauda
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia castanea
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia colombiana
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia manu
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia monohernandezi
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia perspicillata
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia sowelli
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Carollia subrufa
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Platyrrhinus
children.description: Broad-nosed bats — medium-sized fruit bats of Neotropical forests with distinctive white facial stripes and broad, flattened noses. They are important seed dispersers for a wide range of forest fruits and are among the most diverse Phyllostomid genera, with over 20 species spanning lowland Amazonia to Andean cloud forest.

children.name: Platyrrhinus helleri
children.description: A widespread broad-nosed bat of humid lowland forests from Mexico to Bolivia, one of the most commonly captured Platyrrhinus in mist-net surveys. Named after the American zoologist Edmund Heller; it feeds on small to medium fruits and is an important seed disperser in forest interiors and edges.

children.name: Platyrrhinus lineatus
children.description: The most widespread broad-nosed bat in South America, ranging from Venezuela to Argentina in a wide variety of forested and semi-forested habitats. Its bold white facial stripes make it one of the more recognisable Neotropical bats, and it is one of the few phyllostomids adapted to open woodland and gallery forest.

children.name: Platyrrhinus brachycephalus
children.description: A compact, short-skulled broad-nosed bat of Amazonian lowland forest in Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Brazil, inhabiting the interior of primary rainforest. Its short, rounded skull is adapted for processing harder fruits than its longer-faced relatives.

children.name: Platyrrhinus vittatus
children.description: The largest member of the genus, a robust broad-nosed bat of montane forests in the northern Andes and Venezuela, ranging up to 2,500 m elevation. It can handle large, hard-rinded fruits inaccessible to smaller bat species and is an important disperser of large-seeded forest trees.

children.name: Platyrrhinus chocoensis
children.description: Endemic to the Chocó region of western Colombia; pale white dorsal stripe prominent.

children.name: Platyrrhinus dorsobicolor
children.description: Found in the Amazon basin; sharply bicoloured dorsal fur.

children.name: Uroderma
children.description: Tent-making bats — phyllostomids renowned for modifying large leaves into tent-like roosts by biting the lateral veins so the leaf collapses into a protective shelter. A small harem group roosts inside, gaining protection from rain and predators. They were among the first bats documented to engage in this sophisticated roost construction behaviour.

children.name: Uroderma bilobatum
children.description: One of the best-studied tent-making bats, modifying palm, banana, and Heliconia leaves into sheltering roosts that a male defends with a small harem of females. Widespread from Mexico to Bolivia in humid lowland forest, it feeds on figs and soft fruits and is highly adaptable to disturbed habitats.

children.name: Uroderma magnirostrum
children.description: A brown-furred tent-making bat of humid lowland forests from Mexico to Brazil, slightly larger and less commonly encountered than the Common Tent-making Bat. Like its relative, it modifies large leaves into tent roosts and feeds primarily on figs and other small fruits.

children.name: Vampyrodes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Great Stripe-faced Bat, a large, boldly marked fruit bat of Neotropical lowland forest. It is one of the most recognisably marked phyllostomids, with four prominent white facial stripes.

children.name: Vampyrodes caraccioli
children.description: A large, boldly marked fruit bat of humid lowland forests from Mexico to Bolivia, with four conspicuous white facial stripes that make it one of the most distinctive phyllostomids. It feeds on figs and larger forest fruits, roosting in small groups in tent roosts made from large leaves.

children.name: Mesophylla
children.description: MacConnell's Bat — a monotypic genus of small, pale-furred phyllostomid of Amazonian forest. The species is notable for roosting in tent-like modifications of large leaves and for its unusually pale, almost yellowish fur.

children.name: Mesophylla macconnelli
children.description: A small, pale-furred phyllostomid of Amazonian lowland forest, roosting in tent-like leaf modifications and feeding on small fruits. Named after James A. MacConnell; it is typically found in pairs or small groups and is one of the more delicate-looking members of the family.

children.name: Ectophylla
children.description: The Honduran White Bat — arguably the most visually striking of all bats, with snow-white fur and bright yellow nose-leaf, ears, and wing membranes. It is one of only a handful of fully white bats in the world and roosts communally in tent roosts beneath large Heliconia leaves.

children.name: Ectophylla alba
children.description: One of the most visually striking bats in the world, entirely snow-white with vivid yellow nose-leaf, ears, and wing joints. Groups of 6–12 individuals roost beneath a large Heliconia leaf bitten at the lateral veins to form a tent that filters green light, making the white bats appear to glow green — providing camouflage that mimics lichen patches. Restricted to humid lowland forests of Costa Rica, Panama, and adjacent countries.

children.name: Chiroderma
children.description: Big-eyed bats — medium-sized, large-eyed fruit bats of Neotropical lowland and foothill forests. The genus is characterised by prominent eyes, a broad muzzle, and distinct white facial stripes. They feed on figs and other forest fruits and roost in small groups in foliage.

children.name: Chiroderma villosum
children.description: A medium-sized, densely furred big-eyed bat of Amazonian and Atlantic lowland forest, ranging from Costa Rica to Bolivia and Brazil. It feeds on figs and other soft fruits and roosts in small groups in foliage or palm fronds.

children.name: Chiroderma salvini
children.description: A big-eyed bat of humid foothill and montane forests from Mexico to Ecuador, named after the English ornithologist and naturalist Osbert Salvin. It inhabits forest edges and interior at elevations up to 2,000 m, feeding on a wider variety of fruits than the lowland species.

children.name: Chiroderma trinitatum
children.description: The smallest member of the genus, a delicate fruit bat of humid lowland forest from Trinidad to Bolivia. First described from Trinidad, it inhabits primary and mature secondary forest, feeding on small figs and soft fruits in the understorey.

children.name: Vampyressa
children.description: Little yellow-eared bats — small phyllostomids distinguished by yellow-edged ears and a relatively simple noseleaf. They feed on small fruits and roost in foliage in humid lowland forests of Central and South America.

children.name: Vampyressa pusilla
children.description: A tiny fruit bat of Amazonian and Atlantic lowland forest, among the smallest phyllostomids, with distinctively yellow-edged ears. It forages for small fruits and berries in the forest understorey and is rarely encountered in standard mist-net surveys due to its small size and low flight path.

children.name: Vampyressa thyone
children.description: A small yellow-eared bat of humid lowland and foothill forest from Mexico to Ecuador, the northern counterpart of the Southern Little Yellow-eared Bat. It inhabits the understorey of intact lowland forest and requires relatively undisturbed habitat, making it a useful indicator of forest quality.

children.name: Phyllostomus
children.description: Spear-nosed bats — large, omnivorous phyllostomids with unusually broad dietary ranges. Unlike most bats that specialise in one food type, greater spear-nosed bats eat insects, frogs, lizards, mice, other bats, nectar, pollen, and fruit. The erect, spear-like noseleaf gives the family its name. Phyllostomus bats are among the largest Neotropical bats and roost in caves in groups of hundreds to thousands, where they maintain large stable social groups with complex vocalisations that function as group signatures.

children.name: Phyllostomus hastatus
children.description: One of the largest bats in the Americas with a wingspan approaching 50 cm. Harem groups of up to 25 females share a territory. Females produce group-specific 'screech calls' to identify roost-mates in the dark.

children.name: Phyllostomus discolor
children.description: A medium-large omnivorous bat of lowland and foothill forest from Mexico to Bolivia, feeding on insects, fruit, nectar, and occasionally small vertebrates. Distinguished from the Greater Spear-nosed Bat by paler underparts and smaller size; it is one of the most diet-flexible Neotropical bats.

children.name: Phyllostomus elongatus
children.description: A slender, elongated spear-nosed bat of Amazonian lowland forest from Colombia to Bolivia, more insectivorous than most Phyllostomus. Its relatively narrow wings and elongated body reflect a diet that includes a higher proportion of insects than its omnivorous relatives.

children.name: Phyllostomus latifolium
children.description: A medium-sized spear-nosed bat from the Guiana Shield region; feeds on fruit and insects.

children.name: Phyllostomus elongatus
children.description: A medium-sized bat of Amazonian rainforest; elongated lower lip; both fruit and insects.

children.name: Phyllostomus latus
children.description: A large phyllostomid from the Guianas; broad nose-leaf and robust skull.

children.name: Phylloderma
children.description: The Pale-faced Bat — a monotypic genus of large, pale-faced phyllostomid of Amazonian and Central American lowland forest. It is one of the more powerfully built bats in the family, with a broad, short skull adapted for processing hard food items.

children.name: Phylloderma stenops
children.description: A large, powerfully built phyllostomid of humid lowland forest from southern Mexico to Bolivia, feeding on large insects, fruit, and small vertebrates. Its pale face and large eyes are distinctive; it roosts in small groups in tree cavities and caves in primary and mature secondary forest.

children.name: Trachops
children.description: The Fringe-lipped Bat — a specialised predator of frogs that has become a model organism in the study of acoustic prey detection. It eavesdrops on the advertisement calls of male frogs to locate them, distinguishing toxic species from edible ones by their calls alone.

children.name: Trachops cirrhosus
children.description: A celebrated predatory bat of Neotropical lowland forest that eavesdrops on the mating calls of frogs to locate and capture them, even learning to associate novel sounds with prey. It can distinguish toxic from edible frog species by their calls — a remarkable example of acoustic learning in a non-human mammal. Widespread from Mexico to Brazil in humid lowland forest near water.

children.name: Mimon
children.description: Striped hairy-nosed bats — medium-sized insectivore-omnivores of Neotropical forest with large, elaborate noseleafs and ears adapted for detecting low-flying insects near vegetation. They are among the more specialised gleaning insectivores in the family.

children.name: Mimon crenulatum
children.description: A medium-sized gleaning bat of Amazonian and Central American lowland forest, with a large, elaborate horseshoe-shaped noseleaf that enhances echolocation for detecting insects resting on vegetation. It feeds primarily on large arthropods and occasionally small vertebrates, hunting close to the forest understorey.

children.name: Mimon bennettii
children.description: A large, richly coloured hairy-nosed bat of Amazonian lowland forest in Brazil and the Guiana Shield, with golden-brown fur that distinguishes it from other Mimon. A forest-interior specialist that roosts in hollow trees and feeds on large insects and small vertebrates in the forest understorey.

children.name: Lophostoma
children.description: Round-eared bats — phyllostomids of the forest understorey with large, rounded ears adapted for passive listening to detect prey. They feed mainly on large arthropods and small vertebrates, gleaning prey from surfaces with exceptional spatial precision. Some species roost in chambers excavated from active arboreal termite nests.

children.name: Lophostoma silvicolum
children.description: A round-eared bat famous for excavating roosting chambers in active arboreal termite nests, one of the few bats known to actively construct its own roost. A colony of females and young nests inside the termite mound while the occupant termites seal them in nightly; it is found in humid lowland forest from Honduras to Bolivia.

children.name: Lophostoma brasiliense
children.description: The smallest member of the genus, a delicate round-eared bat of lowland Amazonian and Cerrado forest in Brazil, Bolivia, and Peru. It gleans insects from leaves and bark in the forest understorey and is among the less frequently encountered small phyllostomids in standard survey methods.

children.name: Lophostoma evotis
children.description: A small Lophostoma from Central and South America; remarkably large ears for its size.

children.name: Lophostoma schulzi
children.description: Endemic to the Venezuelan Llanos; named after the collector.

children.name: Lampronycteris
children.description: Yellow-throated big-eared bats — a monotypic genus of large-eared phyllostomid with a distinctive bright yellow throat, found in humid lowland forest. It is an understorey gleaning bat that hunts insects close to the forest floor.

children.name: Lampronycteris brachyotis
children.description: A large-eared phyllostomid of humid lowland forest from Mexico to Bolivia, distinguished by its bright yellow throat patch that contrasts with dark brown dorsal fur. It hunts large arthropods by passive listening and gleaning from leaf surfaces in the understorey, roosting in small groups in tree cavities or caves.

children.name: Macrophyllum
children.description: The Long-legged Bat — a highly specialised aquatic insectivore that trawls the surface of forest streams and ponds for insects, using its large hind feet and long legs to rake prey from the water surface — an unusual feeding strategy within Phyllostomidae.

children.name: Macrophyllum macrophyllum
children.description: A unique aquatic-hunting phyllostomid that trawls the surface of forest streams and calm ponds for insects, using its elongated hind feet and legs to rake prey from the water — an ecological role more typical of bulldog bats than leaf-nosed bats. Widespread in humid lowland forest from Mexico to Brazil, it roosts in caves and tree cavities near water.

children.name: Leptonycteris
children.description: Specialist nectar-feeding bats that migrate along corridors of flowering cacti and agaves.

children.name: Leptonycteris curasoae
children.description: A keystone pollinator of columnar cacti in the Andes and Caribbean; undertakes seasonal migrations.

children.name: Leptonycteris nivalis
children.description: Endangered bat pollinating agaves; migrates from Mexico to Texas following flowering pulses.

children.name: Leptonycteris verbabuenae
children.description: A recently described cryptic species within the L. curasoae complex.

children.name: Leptonycteris curasoae
children.description: A nectar bat of the islands of Aruba, Curaçao, and Bonaire. Its long nose and extensible tongue are adapted for feeding on cactus flowers.

children.name: Leptonycteris nivalis
children.description: A migratory bat that follows the blooming of agave plants along the Sierra Madre Oriental. Its population has crashed due to the decline of wild agave.

children.name: Leptonycteris curasoae
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Leptonycteris nivalis
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Leptonycteris yerbabuenae
children.description: A medium-sized species with a typical body plan for its genus. It is active mainly at dusk and dawn, feeding on fruits and leaves.

children.name: Centurio
children.description: A bizarre leaf-nosed bat with extensive facial skin folds that cover the face when resting.

children.name: Centurio senex
children.description: A peculiar bat with retractable facial mask; males have white fur around the neck; feeds on soft fruits.

children.name: Enchistenes
children.description: A distinctive leaf-nosed bat with a long spear-shaped noseleaf; found in Central and South America.

children.name: Enchistenes hartii
children.description: A medium-sized bat with a remarkably elongated noseleaf used in echolocation.

children.name: Vampyrus
children.description: The largest bats in the New World; powerful frugivores with a distinctive facial structure.

children.name: Vampyrus spectrum
children.description: The largest bat in the New World; wingspan up to 1 m; preys on birds and small mammals.

children.name: Vampyrus biceps
children.description: A smaller relative of the Spectral Bat; restricted to the Amazon basin.
